primary_diagnosis.name: Severe plaque psoriasis, acute exacerbation, with systemic inflammatory fatigue

pathophysiology_summary: Long-standing chronic plaque psoriasis is an immune-mediated (IL-23 / Th17 driven) hyperproliferative skin disease. This is an acute severe exacerbation precipitated by abrupt cessation/undertreatment of high-potency topical corticosteroid three days ago on a background of high psychosocial stress — both recognised flare triggers. Rapid plaque expansion with fissuring breaches the epidermal barrier (pain, bleeding, portal for secondary infection and fluid/heat loss). The profound fatigue and skin tightness reflect the systemic inflammatory burden of extensive active disease (~35% BSA), not a separate complex-fatigue entity. The acute concern in the source note — the need to exclude generalized pustular psoriasis and superimposed bacterial cellulitis/sepsis — is answered NEGATIVELY on the patient-obtainable data (afebrile 37.4 C, no visible sterile pustules, bilateral symmetric distribution, no localised hot spreading unilateral area). Definitive control requires dermatology-directed systemic therapy; topical corticosteroid plus emollient is an appropriate bridging rescue.

differential.evidence_basis: Known long-standing psoriasis with rapid expansion of thick scaly fissuring plaques over 48 hours — a classic severe flare.
A dermatological emergency that can be precipitated by abrupt topical/systemic steroid withdrawal — must be actively screened for even though not yet suggested.
Open fissures are a portal for infection; cellulitis/sepsis must be excluded before attributing everything to the flare.
Widespread eczematous/inflamed skin can rarely be complicated by disseminated HSV — screen for punched-out vesicles and disproportionate deterioration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should place the psoriasis flare leading while explicitly holding generalized pustular psoriasis and superimposed cellulitis as important-not-to-miss to be screened — not jump straight to emergency escalation, and not dismiss the mimics.

differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, no visible sterile pustules, bilateral symmetric distribution, no unilateral hot spreading area — the flare is confirmed as the cause and the emergent mimics are screened negative on patient-obtainable data.
No sterile pustules or lakes of pus and no high fever on patient report — currently screened negative, but the steroid-withdrawal trigger keeps it on the safety-net.
Afebrile and bilateral symmetric distribution with no single hot spreading focus argue against active cellulitis now, but the open fissures keep secondary infection on the safety-net.
Fissured, bleeding, weeping plaques are a portal for infection — no current signs (no honey crust, no purulence reported) but a live safety-net risk.
No punched-out painful vesicles and no rapid deterioration disproportionate to plaque morphology — reasonably excluded on history.
No active joint swelling reported and no synovitis on the source in-person joint exam — not the driver of this presentation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the emergency screen the flare should be firmly leading with the dangerous mimics screened negative but retained as explicit safety-net items — this is the pivot the case tests: recognise the benign chronic cause without discarding the emergencies.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe exacerbation of known plaque psoriasis after steroid withdrawal and stress, with a negative acute red-flag screen — managed with topical rescue plus a prompt dermatology/systemic pathway and explicit safety-netting.
Retained on the safety-net given the steroid-withdrawal trigger — escalate immediately if pustulation or fever/rigors develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position is a recognised severe plaque psoriasis flare at a prompt-GP tier with mandatory escalation triggers for pustulation, fever/rigors, spreading hot erythema and systemic compromise. Reflexive ED/000 for this stable afebrile flare is over-triage; discarding the GPP/sepsis safety-net is under-triage.

red_flags.description: High fever with rigors/shivering (systemic upset — generalized pustular psoriasis or secondary sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever with rigors on a background of extensive active/fissured psoriasis signals systemic pustular psoriasis or bacteraemia from secondary infection — an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Evolving pustular psoriasis or secondary sepsis is not escalated; patient deteriorates at home.

red_flags.description: Sudden widespread sterile pustules / lakes of pus (generalized pustular psoriasis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Coalescing sterile pustules define generalized pustular psoriasis — a dermatological emergency needing urgent in-person and specialist care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Pustular transformation is missed; delay risks fluid loss, sepsis and cardiorespiratory instability.

red_flags.description: One area becoming hot, unilateral, rapidly spreading, red-streaked or with purulent discharge (cellulitis / secondary infection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A localised hot spreading area or streaking indicates cellulitis/lymphangitis superimposed on the barrier breach — needs urgent assessment and antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Secondary cellulitis progresses to systemic sepsis untreated.

red_flags.description: Extensive fissured, bleeding, weeping plaques (~35% BSA) — barrier breach with secondary-infection and fluid-loss risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Widespread barrier failure is the present risk driver — it warrants prompt (not routine) review and close safety-netting even though it is not itself an immediate emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciated barrier failure leads to secondary infection, dehydration or progression before review.

red_flags.description: Abrupt cessation of high-potency topical corticosteroid (undertreatment / rebound trigger)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Abrupt steroid withdrawal both drove this flare and raises the risk of rebound to pustular or erythrodermic psoriasis — it must inform management (do NOT reach for systemic steroids) and safety-netting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The reversible trigger is missed and a systemic steroid may be wrongly given, risking rebound pustular/erythrodermic disease.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The 'urgent on-site assessment mandated' framing and dramatic visual severity pressure the AI Doctor to escalate a stable afebrile flare straight to ED/000 rather than recognising a known chronic condition manageable with prompt GP + dermatology and safety-netting.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on systemic fatigue as a separate entity
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the profound fatigue as an independent complex-fatigue syndrome to be worked up, rather than as inflammatory-burden fatigue that will settle as the plaque flare is controlled.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Commission bias — systemic steroids as rescue
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reaching for oral/systemic corticosteroid to 'rescue' the flare; in psoriasis this risks rebound generalized pustular or erythrodermic disease on withdrawal and is the wrong lever.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature exclusion of the emergent mimics
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing generalized pustular psoriasis and superimposed cellulitis without actively screening (fever/rigors, pustules, unilateral hot spreading area) — the correct move is screen-and-retain-on-safety-net, not discard.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True body-surface-area involvement and severity of plaque disease
Whether early sterile pustules are forming (requires full skin examination)
Presence of deep soft-tissue infection, fluctuance, lymphangitic streaking or purulence
Objective vital signs and haemodynamic status beyond patient-reported values
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review within 24-48 hours for skin examination, a dermatology referral and consideration of systemic therapy; escalate immediately to ED/000 only if fever with rigors, sudden widespread pustulation, a hot spreading unilateral area/streaking, or systemic compromise develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: over-calling a dermatological emergency and escalating a stable afebrile flare to ED/000 (over-triage), or falsely reassuring and omitting the generalized-pustular-psoriasis / secondary-sepsis safety-net (under-triage).

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: High-potency topical corticosteroid plus emollient is appropriate for a plaque psoriasis flare; extensive/severe or unstable disease warrants dermatology referral for systemic therapy. Avoid systemic corticosteroids for chronic plaque psoriasis owing to rebound (pustular/erythrodermic) risk on withdrawal.
